5 Common React Native Mistakes That Slow Down Your App (And How to Fix Them)
Performance is one of the biggest factors that determines whether users love or abandon a mobile application. Even with a beautiful UI and powerful features, a sluggish app can quickly lead to poor reviews and lower user retention.
The good news? Most performance issues come from a handful of common development mistakes that are easy to fix.
Here are five React Native mistakes every developer should avoid.
1. Using ScrollView for Large Lists
One of the most common mistakes is rendering long lists using ScrollView.
ScrollView renders every child component at once, which increases memory usage and slows down the application as the list grows.
Instead, use FlatList, which only renders the visible items on the screen.
Best Practices

• Use initialNumToRender

• Configure windowSize

• Enable removeClippedSubviews

• Implement getItemLayout when possible
These optimizations significantly improve scrolling performance.
2. Causing Unnecessary Re-renders
Every unnecessary render means extra work for React Native.
Large applications often suffer because components re-render even when their data hasn’t changed.
You can reduce this by using:

• React.memo()

• useMemo()

• useCallback()
Optimizing renders leads to smoother navigation and faster UI updates.
3. Loading Large Unoptimized Images
Images are one of the biggest contributors to poor application performance.
Uploading high-resolution assets directly into your application increases loading time, memory consumption, and bundle size.
Instead:

• Compress images before adding them.

• Resize images to the required dimensions.

• Prefer modern image formats when supported.

• Lazy load heavy assets whenever possible.
Small optimizations can dramatically improve perceived performance.
4. Creating Inline Functions Everywhere
Inline functions are convenient but create a new function every time a component renders.
For example:
<TouchableOpacity onPress={() => handlePress()} />A better approach is using useCallback for functions that don't need to be recreated on every render.
This helps reduce unnecessary renders, especially when passing callbacks to child components.
5. Ignoring FlatList Optimization
Simply switching from ScrollView to FlatList isn’t enough.
Many developers leave FlatList with its default configuration, missing out on important performance improvements.
Consider configuring:

• initialNumToRender

• maxToRenderPerBatch

• windowSize

• keyExtractor

• removeClippedSubviews
These settings help your application remain smooth even with thousands of items.
Bonus Tips
Here are a few additional ways to improve React Native performance:

• Enable Hermes.

• Remove unused dependencies.

• Keep components modular.

• Optimize API requests.

• Test using production builds.

• Profile your application with Flipper or React Native DevTools.
Final Thoughts
Performance optimization isn’t something you do at the end of a project — it’s something you build into your development process from day one.
Avoiding these five common mistakes can significantly improve your application’s responsiveness, reduce memory usage, and create a better experience for users.
Every small optimization adds up, and over time those improvements create applications that feel polished, reliable, and professional.
